title: pip and Third-Party Packages
subtitle: Discover Python's enormous ecosystem of ready-made libraries
learning_objectives: Understand what PyPI is and what pip does
Know how to install a package with pip
Recognise the most popular Python packages and what they do

sections.heading: What is pip and PyPI?
sections.content:
PyPI (Python Package Index) is the official online repository for Python packages — over 500,000 free libraries created by the Python community. pip is Python's package manager — it downloads and installs packages from PyPI.

In a terminal, you install a package like this:
pip install requests

Then in your code: import requests
sections.code_example:
# After 'pip install requests', you would write:
# import requests
# response = requests.get('https://api.github.com')
# print(response.status_code)  # 200 means OK

# After 'pip install pandas', you would write:
# import pandas as pd
# data = pd.read_csv('data.csv')
# print(data.head())

# We can't use these in the browser, but you'll use them
# when writing Python on your own computer!
print("pip makes Python infinitely powerful!")
sections.note: In this browser environment, most third-party packages are not available. But on a real computer, pip install gives you access to hundreds of thousands of tools.

sections.heading: Essential Python Packages to Know
sections.content:
Here are the most important third-party packages every Python developer should know about:

requests — make HTTP web requests (fetch web pages, call APIs)
numpy — fast number crunching and arrays (used in science/ML)
pandas — data analysis with tables (like Excel in Python)
matplotlib — create charts and graphs
flask / django — build websites and web APIs
pillow — edit images (resize, crop, filters)
pytest — test your code automatically
black — automatically format your code
sections.code_example:
# Virtual environments keep project dependencies separate
# Create one: python3 -m venv myenv
# Activate it: source myenv/bin/activate (Linux/Mac)
#              myenv\Scripts\activate (Windows)
# Install packages: pip install requests pandas
# Save requirements: pip freeze > requirements.txt
# Install from file: pip install -r requirements.txt

print("Python's ecosystem is enormous!")
print("There is almost certainly a package for what you need.")
sections.code_output:
Python's ecosystem is enormous!
There is almost certainly a package for what you need.

exercises.title: What You Would Install
exercises.prompt: Print a list of 4 packages and what they do. Use print() for each one. (This exercises what you learned, not actual pip usage.)
exercises.starter_code:
# Print 4 Python packages and what they do
# Format: "package_name: what it does"
exercises.solution:
print("requests: makes HTTP web requests")
print("pandas: data analysis with tables")
print("flask: build web applications")
print("pillow: edit and process images")
hints: The starter code is already correct — just run it!

exercises.title: Use the Standard Library Instead
exercises.prompt: Without any pip installs, use the json module (standard library) to convert a Python dict to a JSON string and back. Print both.
exercises.starter_code:
import json

person = {"name": "Alice", "age": 30, "city": "London"}

# Convert the person dict to a JSON string and print it

# Parse the JSON string back to a dict and print the "name" value
exercises.solution:
import json

person = {"name": "Alice", "age": 30, "city": "London"}

json_string = json.dumps(person)
print(json_string)

parsed = json.loads(json_string)
print(parsed["name"])
hints: The starter code is complete — just run it!
json.loads() parses string back to dict

quiz.question: What is PyPI?
options: The official online repository of Python packages
quiz.explanation: PyPI (Python Package Index) at pypi.org is the official repository where Python developers publish free, reusable packages. pip downloads packages from PyPI.

quiz.question: Which package would you use for data analysis with tables (like Excel in Python)?
quiz.explanation: pandas is the go-to package for data analysis. It provides a DataFrame structure (like a spreadsheet table) and tools to read CSV/Excel files, filter data, compute statistics, and much more.

completion_message: You have completed the Modules and Libraries module! You are ready to use the entire Python ecosystem!
